title: 1. Download and process NLDAS
text: article describes downloading processing total incoming shortwave radiation (W m-2) using StreamLightUtils package. Total incoming shortwave radiation (W m-2) provided National Land Data Assimilation System (NLDAS) hourly timesteps 0.125 degree spatial resolution. two potential workflows available: 1.) download data single site, 2.) bulk download data many sites.

title: 1. Download and process NLDAS
text: Previously, packaged used NASA data rods service download NLDAS data. However, service retired new workflow required. Downloading NLDAS data now requires Earthdata login. already account, can register new Earthdata account. account, may login account. account page, click “generate token” tab paste token string txt file save somewhere locally token.txt. Note please careful save token make sure accidentally include projects managed places like GitHub. Unfortunately, generate new token every 60 days expire. can read access token R, modifying  reflect path token saved.
code: #Read in Earthdata access token   access_token <- readLines("<path_to_token>/token.txt")

what: 1. Downloading and processing NLDAS data for a single site
title: 1. Download and process NLDAS
text: Incoming shortwave radiation data single site can downloaded using function nldas_get following structure: nldas_get(Site_ID, Lat, Lon, startDate, endDate, access_token, save_dir, ) Site_ID site identifier (“Site_ID”) Lat site latitude Lon site longitude startDate starting date downloaded data (“YYYY-MM-DD”) endDate ending date downloaded data (“YYYY-MM-DD”). Default current date via Sys.Date() access_token Earthdata access token. Requires Earthdata profile generate token. save_dir save directory downloaded data placed . example, “C:/” download light formatting done extract relevant date time information. downloaded data can prepped creating model driver file using function nldas_prep following structure: nldas_prep(read_dir, write_output, save_dir) read_dir directory containing downloaded NLDAS data. write_output logical value indicating whether output written disk (write_output = TRUE) returned R environment (write_output = FALSE). default value FALSE since datasets suitable approach; however, large datasets (thousands sites) may easier write files disk instead storing workspace. save_dir optional parameter use write_output = TRUE indicates save directory files placed . example, “C:/”. Note, nldas_prep creating named list downloaded NLDAS data, names site ids. done faciliate later steps creating model driver file.
code: library("StreamLightUtils")      #Download NLDAS data at NC_NHC   nldas_get(     Site_ID = "NC_NHC",     Lat = 35.9925,      Lon = -79.0460,          startDate = "2022-01-01",     endDate = "2024-12-31",     access_token = access_token,     save_dir = here::here("data", "nldas")   )    #Prep the downloaded NLDAS data for creating a model driver file   nldas_prepped <- nldas_prep(     read_dir = here::here("data", "nldas")   )

what: 2. Downloading and processing NLDAS data for multiple sites
title: 1. Download and process NLDAS
text: Incoming shortwave radiation data multiple sites can downloaded using function nldas_get_bulk following structure: nldas_get_bulk(site_locs, startDate, endDate, access_token, save_dir, ) site_locs table Site_ID, Lat, Lon, optionally startDate endDate. startDate optional parameter. default, nothing provided function assumes site_locs column contains startDate. Alternatively, single startDate can provided argument download (YYYY-MM-DD). endDate optional parameter. default, nothing provided function assumes site_locs column contains endDate. Alternatively, single endDate can provided argument download (YYYY-MM-DD). access_token Earthdata access token. Requires Earthdata profile generate token. save_dir save directory downloaded data placed . example, “C:/”. function can essentially used two ways: 1.) can specify arguments startDate endDate, may helpful want download timeframe data sites, 2.) specify columns startDate endDate file pass site_locs, helpful want different timeframes data site. data fails download site, nldas_get_bulk function automatically check retry downloading data sites missing data. However, possible data exist site. Consequently, prudent confirm successfully downloaded sites.
code: #Read in a table with initial site information   data("NC_params", package = "StreamLight")    sites <- NC_params  #Download NLDAS data at NC_NHC   nldas_get_bulk(     site_locs = sites,     startDate = "2022-01-01",     endDate = "2024-12-31",     access_token = access_token,     save_dir = here::here("data", "nldas")   )  #Prep the downloaded NLDAS data for creating a model driver file   nldas_prepped <- nldas_prep(     read_dir = here::here("data", "nldas")   )

title: 2. Download and process MODIS LAI
text: article covers downloading processing MODIS LAI data using StreamLightUtils package. variety sources LAI data, convenience function included StreamLightUtils process two MODIS LAI products: 1.) MCD15A3H.061 4 day temporal resolution pixel size 500m, 2.) MCD15A2H.061 8 day temporal resolution pixel size 500m. example uses MCD15A3H data downloaded process either product. products downloaded AppEEARS website.

title: 2. Download and process MODIS LAI
text: functions StreamLightUtils based accessing LAI data AppEEARS website. requires NASA EARTHDATA account access already account may register . submit request, need table basic site information. .csv Site_ID, Lat Lon can used submit request extracts point samples multiple locations. registered exported table site information, can begin making request: Click Extract > Point Sample top menu bar next page select Start new request NC_sites.csv exported can now simply drag--dropped populate list sites. may notice special characters like “_” removed ID field, don’t worry ok. Next, begin filling remaining information submit request including name, Start Date, End Date, Selected layers. possible, advisable select dates time either side desired period estimate light. help constrain LAI processing steps interpolating daily values. example, wanted run model 2023 might download LAI data 2022-2024. Type MCD15A3H layers search box select product. Add layers selected layers. Drag drop site locations Example request submission everything filled submit request. receive email notification request second notification download ready. Downloads typically ready day within day two depending size request. reference, request example took minutes complete.
code: #For this example, we will use the example parameter file provided with the package   data("NC_params", package = "StreamLight")    sites <- NC_params  #Make a table for the MODIS request    request_sites <- sites[, c("Site_ID", "Lat", "Lon")]             #Export your sites as a .csv for the AppEEARS request     write.table(     request_sites,      here::here("data", "modis", "NC_sites.csv"),      sep = ",",      row.names = FALSE,     quote = FALSE,      col.names = FALSE   )

title: 2. Download and process MODIS LAI
text: download ready can processed using two built-functions StreamLightUtils. downloaded .zip file can unpacked using appeears_unpack_qc following structure: appeears_unpack_qc(zip_file, zip_dir, request_sites) zip_file name zip file. example, “myzip.zip” zip_dir directory zip file located . example, “C:/” request_sites string site IDs function returns unpacked data list, element list representing data given site.
code: mod_unpack <- appeears_unpack_qc(   zip_file = "nc_sites.zip",    zip_dir = here::here("data", "modis"),    request_sites[, "Site_ID"] )

title: 2. Download and process MODIS LAI
text: StreamLightUtils package leverages phenofit package help handling processing LAI data. variety curve fitting methods tutorial uses approach Gu et al. (2009) unpacked data can processed using appeears_prep following structure: appeears_prep(Site, proc_type) unpacked_LAI Output appeears_unpack_qc function fit_method several options available phenofit package including “AG”, “Beck”, “Elmore”, “Gu”, “Klos”, “Zhang”. write_ouput Logical indicating whether write individual driver file disk. Default value FALSE. save_dir Optional parameter write_output = TRUE. save directory files placed . example, “C:/ plot Logical, plot = TRUE generates plot plot = FALSE Let’s process LAI data visualize results. black line new fitted, interpolated, daily LAI.
code: mod_prepped <- appeears_prep(   unpacked_LAI = mod_unpack,     fit_method = "Gu",    plot = TRUE )

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: article demonstrate creating estimates photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) using stream_light function. details application model detailed Savoy et al. (2021). Outline Overview: General overview function structure. Preparing driver file: Assembling timeseries model drivers fed model. Preparing parameter file: Creating parameter file describes various site conditions. Running stream_light: Example code run model.

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: First, let’s take look stream_light function following structure: stream_light(driver_file, Lat, Lon, channel_azimuth, bottom_width, BH, BS, WL, TH, overhang_height, x_LAD) driver_file model driver file Lat site latitude Lon site longitude channel_azimuth Channel azimuth bottom_width Bottom width (m) BH Bank height (m) BS Bank slope WL Water level (m) TH Tree height (m) overhang Maximum canopy overhang (m) overhang_height Height maximum canopy overhang (m). overhang_height = NA, model defaults value 75% tree height. x_LAD Leaf angle distribution, default = 1 Running stream_light model requires parameter file describes various site characteristics driver file contains inputs model. first argument function (driver_file) standardized model driver file contains total incoming irradiance (W m-2) leaf area index (LAI) (m2 m-2) used model inputs. remaining arguments function parameters describe site characteristics. surface seems like large number parameters;however, tutorial provides indepth information parameters simplifying assumptions can used reduce number necessary parameters.

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: two necessary components drive stream_light. First, incoming canopy total irradiance (W m-2) needed input radiative transfer model. Second, daily estimates LAI needed determine attenuation light canopies within radiative transfer model. set functions included StreamLightUtils package help create standardized model driver file. structure model driver follows: “local_time”: POSIXct object local time “offset”: UTC offset local time (hours), used solar_c function “jday”: unique identifier day combines year day year information format YYYYddd “DOY”: day year (1-365 366 leap years) “Hour”: Hour day (0-23) “SW_inc”: Total incoming downwelling shortwave radiation (W m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools get hourly data NLDAS. “LAI”: MODIS leaf area index (m2 m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools generate interpolated daily values using AppEEARS_proc function. driver file structure can made using make_driver function StreamLightUtils following structure: make_driver(site_locs, NLDAS_processed, MOD_processed, write_output, save_dir) site_locs table Site_ID, Lat, Lon, coordinate reference system designated EPSG code. example, common geographic system WGS84 EPSG code 4326 nldas_prepped Output nldas_prep function (StreamLightUtils) modis_prepped Output appeears_prep function (StreamLightUtils) write_output Logical value indicate whether write individual driver file disk. Default value FALSE. save_dir Optional parameter write_output = TRUE. save directory files placed . example, “C:/ Let’s take moment examine final structure driver file
code: #Read in a driver file   data("NC_NHC_driver", package = "StreamLight")   head(NC_NHC_driver)

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: several site parameters required run stream_light; however, parameters built functions within StreamLightUtils. Similarly, parameters easily obtained equal importance model performance. , detail process used extract parameter values Savoy et al. (2021). begin , let’s revisit parameters used: Lat site latitude Lon site longitude channel_azimuth Channel azimuth bottom_width Bottom width (m) BH Bank height (m) BS Bank slope WL Water level (m) TH Tree height (m) overhang Maximum canopy overhang (m) overhang_height Height maximum canopy overhang (m) x_LAD Leaf angle distribution run model multiple sites easiest construct table parameters site following example.

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: Currently functionality derive stream azimuth within StreamLightUtils. meantime, can derived manually using aerial photographs, flowlines, field derived measurements. based model SHADE2 (Li et al., 2012), follow conventions stream azimuth measured clockwise North (see figure ). However, present banks parameterized identically StreamLight (e.g. single tree height put instead heights trees either bank) reality channel azimuth 45\(^\circ\) 225\(^\circ\) yield results. mention point case future development may allow parameterizing banks separately, case someone wanted modify code add functionality. Example deriving azimuth, note first azimuth first example 45\(^\circ\) whereas second example 315\(^\circ\).

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: StreamLightUtils built function derive tree height using LiDAR derived estimates Simard et al. (2011). function extract_height retrieve estimate tree height (m) based latitude longitude following structure: extract_height(Site_ID, Lat, Lon) Site_ID site identifier (“Site_ID”) Lat site latitude Lon site longitude site_crs coordinate reference system points, preferably designated EPSG code. example, common geographic system WGS84 EPSG code 4326. Although parameter file already contains tree height, following example use funciton
code: #Extract tree height   extract_height(     Site_ID = "NC_NHC",      Lat = 35.9925,     Lon = -79.046,     site_crs = 4326   )  #Or iterate over multiple sites   mapply(     FUN = extract_height,     Site_ID = NC_params[, "Site_ID"],      Lat = NC_params[, "Lat"],     Lon = NC_params[, "Lon"],     site_crs = NC_params[, "epsg_crs"],     simard2011 = simard2011,     SIMPLIFY = TRUE   ) |>     t() |>     as.data.frame()

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: First time installation StreamLight package GitHub can done devtools library installed, package can loaded normal. Estimates average light across transect can estimated using stream_light function following structure: stream_light(driver_file, Lat, Lon, channel_azimuth, bottom_width, BH, BS, WL, TH, overhang_height, x_LAD) driver_file model driver file Lat site latitude Lon site longitude channel_azimuth Channel azimuth bottom_width Bottom width (m) BH Bank height (m) BS Bank slope WL Water level (m) TH Tree height (m) overhang Maximum canopy overhang (m) overhang_height Height maximum canopy overhang (m). overhang_height = NA, model defaults value 75% tree height. x_LAD Leaf angle distribution, default = 1 outlined previous section preparing parameter files. Savoy et al. (2021) made simplifying assumptions facilitate applying model easily locations lacked detailed situ measurements.
code: devtools::install_github("psavoy/StreamLight") library("StreamLight")

what: Generate estimates for a single site
title: 3. Using stream_light
text: run model single site simply add parameters function.
code: #Load the example driver file for NC_NHC   data("NC_NHC_driver", package = "StreamLight")  #Run the model   NC_NHC_modeled <- stream_light(     NC_NHC_driver,      Lat = 35.9925,      Lon = -79.0460,      channel_azimuth = 330,      bottom_width = 18.9,      BH = 0.1,      BS = 100,      WL = 0.1,      TH = 23,      overhang = 2.3,      overhang_height = NA,      x_LAD = 1   )

title: 3. Using stream_light
text: also possible loop multiple sites wrapping model another function example adapted workflow. columns follows: “local_time”: POSIXct object local time “offset”: UTC offset local time (hours), used solar_c function “jday”: unique identifier day combines year day year information format YYYYddd “DOY”: day year (1-365 366 leap years) “Hour”: Hour day (0-23) “SW_inc”: Total incoming downwelling shortwave radiation (W m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools get hourly data NLDAS. “LAI”: MODIS leaf area index (m2 m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools generate interpolated daily values using AppEEARS_proc function. “PAR_inc”: Incoming PAR canopy (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “PAR_bc”: Estimated PAR (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) directly canopy “veg_shade”: proportion channel crossection shaded riparian vegetation “bank_shade”: proportion channel crossection shaded stream banks “PAR_stream”: estimated PAR channel cross section (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1)
code: #Function for batching over multiple sites   batch_model <- function(Site, parameters, read_dir){     #Get the model driver       driver_file <- readRDS(here::here(read_dir, paste0(Site, "_driver.rds")))            #Get model parameters for the site       site_p <- parameters[parameters[, "Site_ID"] == Site, ]      #Run the model       modeled <- stream_light(         driver_file,          Lat = site_p[, "Lat"],          Lon = site_p[, "Lon"],         channel_azimuth = site_p[, "Azimuth"],          bottom_width = site_p[, "Width"],          BH = site_p[, "BH"],         BS = site_p[, "BS"],          WL = site_p[, "WL"],          TH = site_p[, "TH"],          overhang = site_p[, "overhang"],         overhang_height = site_p[, "overhang_height"],          x_LAD = site_p[, "x"]       )      return(modeled)    } #End batch_model   #Running the model   modeled_light <- lapply(     NC_params[, "Site_ID"],      FUN = batch_model,      parameters = NC_params,     read_dir = here::here("output")   )      names(modeled_light) <- NC_params[, "Site_ID"]  #Take a look at the output   data("NC_NHC_predicted", package = "StreamLight")   NC_NHC_predicted[1:2, ]

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: article demonstrate creating estimates photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) using aqua_light function. details application model detailed Savoy & Harvey. (2021a), modeled estimates paper available ScienceBase data release (Savoy & Harvey, 2021b). many ways derive values site parameters model drivers; however, document focuses approaches used Savoy & Harvey. (2021a) give exhaustive set possibilities. Outline Overview: General overview function structure. Preparing driver file: Assembling timeseries model drivers fed model. Preparing parameter file: Creating parameter file describes various site conditions. Running aqua_light: Example code run model generate estimates data release brief explanation model outputs. Appendix: Governing equations light reflection attenuation function depth clarity.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: tutorial cover basic model inputs, model structure, model outputs. First, let’s take look aqua_light function following structure: aqua_light(driver_file, Lat, Lon, channel_azimuth, bankfull_width, BH, TH, overhang, overhang_height, x_LAD) driver_file model driver file Lat site latitude Lon site longitude channel_azimuth Channel azimuth bankfull width Bankfull width (m) BH Bankfull height (m) TH Tree height (m) overhang Maximum canopy overheight (m) overhang_height Height maximum canopy overhang (m). overhang_height = NA, model defaults value 75% tree height. x_LAD Leaf angle distribution, default = 1 Running aqua_light model requires parameter file describes various site characteristics driver file contains inputs model. first argument function (driver_file) standardized model driver file contains timeseries model inputs. remaining arguments function parameters describe site characteristics. Section 2 gives information creating model driver file section 3 details creation site parameter file.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: structure model driver follows: “local_time”: Local time format YYYY-mm-dd HH:MM:SS based site timezone “offset”: UTC offset local time (hours) “jday”: unique identifier day combines year day year information format YYYYddd “Year” year “DOY”: day year (1-365 366 leap years) “Hour”: Hour day (0-23) “SW_inc”: Total incoming downwelling shortwave radiation (W m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools get hourly data NLDAS. “LAI”: MODIS leaf area index (m2 m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools generate interpolated daily values using AppEEARS_proc function. “depth”: Water depth (m) “width”: Wetted width (m) “kd_pred”: irradiance attenuation coefficient (m -1) Preparing driver file may depend available data site needs individual researchers. StreamLightUtils package provides utilities generate standardized driver file contains “local_time”, “offset”, “jday”, “Year”, “DOY”, “Hour”, “SW_inc”, LAI. following sections provide information approaches used Savoy & Harvey. (2021a) derive “depth”, “width”, “kd_pred”.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Depth measured estimated variety ways, one possibility estimate depth function discharge hydraulic geometry coefficients following Gomez-Velez et al. (2015) (depth = c * Qf), Q discharge (m3 s-1) terms c f empirical coefficients. hydraulic geometry coefficients Gomez-Velez et al. (2015) included Appling et al. (2018) 356 U.S. rivers; however, since Appling et al. (2018) already done conversion used values. depths represent mean depth averaged reach length width date (4am 3:59pm).

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Similar depth, estimated width function discharge hydraulic geometry coefficients following Gomez-Velez et al. (2015) (width = * Qb), Q discharge (m3 s-1) terms b empirical coefficients. used mean daily discharge calculate mean width date (4am 3:59pm).

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: get timeseries irradiance attenuation coefficients (Kd) developed empirical log-log linear relationship turbidity (FNU) Kd: \[ log(K_d) = 0.52 * log(turbidity) - 0.26 \]

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Currently functionality derive stream azimuth within StreamLightUtils package. meantime, can derived manually using aerial photographs, flowlines, field derived measurements. functions used calculate shading (Li et al., 2012) follow convention stream azimuth measured clockwise North. However, since banks parameterized identically stream_light *aqua_light, distinction less relevant azimuth 45\(^{\circ}\) 225\(^\circ\) yield results. mention point case future development may allow parameterizing banks separately, case someone wanted modify code add functionality. Example deriving azimuth, note first azimuth first example 45\(^\circ\) whereas second example 315\(^\circ\).

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: estimated bankfull channel width regionalized hydraulic geometry coefficients function discharge (Gomez-Velez et al. 2015). Bankfull widths also acquired field measurements several remotely-sensed empirically-derived products.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: estimated bank heights based maximum predicted water depth site, field surveys LiDAR data alternative sources derive parameter.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Tree heights derived global estimates canopy heights 30m resolution Potapov et al. (2021). 90th percentile tree height within 60m buffer riparian zone calculated site. Alternatively, users can use extract_height function StreamLightUtils package extract 1km LiDAR data Simard et al. (2011) based latitude longitude.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Without detailed information canopy overhang assumed overhang 10% tree height sites. Savoy et al. (2021) 10% overhang used validate stream_light function; however, rather simple assumption. Savoy et al. (2021) gives suggestions potential data sources used refine estimates.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Without detailed information height maximum canopy overhang value NA can used. overhang_height = NA, model default using 75% tree height.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: canopies can approximated spherical distribution leaf angles (x = 1) (Campbell & Norman, 1998), x_LAD set 1 sites.

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: part ScienceBase data release (Savoy & Harvey, 2021b), prepared R environment (loaded_environment.RData) contains necessary inputs outputs aqua_light. data used demonstrate use aqua_light. beginning necessary download load prepared R environment (loaded_environment.RData) using load command. example file located C:/ directory: easily run model across many sites wrapper function included . get relevant parameters driver files site return modeled estimates next step simply apply function across sites. , model drivers placed single list easily loop site driver file. modeled output contains following columns: “local_time”: Local time format YYYY-mm-dd HH:MM:SS based site timezone “offset”: UTC offset local time (hours) “jday”: unique identifier day combines year day year information format YYYYddd “Year” year “DOY”: day year (1-365 366 leap years) “Hour”: Hour day (0-23) “SW_inc”: Total incoming downwelling shortwave radiation (W m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools get hourly data NLDAS. Specifically, data used example North American Land Data Assimilation System (NLDAS) (original attribute name DSWRFsfc) . data downloaded processed using StreamLightUtils package. “LAI”: MODIS leaf area index (m2 m-2). StreamLightUtils provides tools generate interpolated daily values using AppEEARS_proc function. Specifically, data used example MODIS 8-day 500m leaf area index (MCD15A2H-006). data interpolated daily values following method Gu et al. (2009) using StreamLightUtils package, leverages routines phenofit R package. “depth”: Water depth (m). Specifically, data used example mean depth (m), averaged reach length width, date (4am 3:59pm) Appling et al. (2018). “width”: Wetted width (m). Specifically, data used example mean width (m) date (4am 3:59pm) calculated mean discharge equation estimate width function discharge (width = * Qb) Gomez-Velez et al. (2015). “kd_pred”: predicted irradiance attenuation coefficient (Kd, m-1) turbid water. , predicted based current turbidity using log-log linear regression Kd turbidity derived situ measurements. “PAR_inc”: Incoming, canopy, PAR (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “veg_shade”: proportion channel crossection shaded riparian vegetation “bank_shade”: proportion channel crossection shaded stream banks “PAR_surface”: Estimated PAR stream surface (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “PAR_subsurface”: Estimated PAR stream surface (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “PAR_water”: Estimated PAR benthic surface assuming optically clear water (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “kd_water”: Irradiance attenuation coefficient optically clear water “PAR_turb”: Estimated PAR benthic surface turbid water (\(\mu\)mol m-2 s-1) “kd_turb”: irradiance attenuation coefficient turbid water. value predicted based current turbidity using log-log linear regression Kd turbidity derived situ measurements. *Note, modeled predictions PAR NA values sun horizon.
code: load("C:/loaded_environment.RData") batch_model <- function(Site_ID, model_parameters, model_drivers){   #Print a message to keep track of progress     message(paste0("Generating modeled estimates for ", Site_ID))          #Get the model driver     driver_file <- model_drivers[[Site_ID]]          #The input_output file contains the model inputs and outputs, let's select just the necessary   #input columns to reduce confusion     model_driver <- driver_file[, c("local_time", "offset", "jday", "Year", "DOY", "Hour", "SW_inc",       "LAI", "kd_pred", "depth", "width")]       #Get model parameters for the site     site_p <- model_parameters[model_parameters[, "Site_ID"] == Site_ID, ]        #Run the model     modeled <- aqua_light(       driver_file = model_driver,        Lat = site_p[, "Lat"],        Lon = site_p[, "Lon"],       channel_azimuth = site_p[, "Azimuth"],        bankfull_width = site_p[, "width_harvey"],        BH = site_p[, "bank_height"],       TH = site_p[, "TH"],        overhang = site_p[, "overhang"],       overhang_height = site_p[, "overhang_height"],       x_LAD = site_p[, "x"]     )        return(modeled)        } #End batch_model modeled_estimates <- lapply(   names(inputs_outputs),   FUN = batch_model,   model_parameters = site_parameters,   model_drivers = inputs_outputs )

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: aqua_light adds several proceses existing stream_light function including water surface reflection attenuation function depth clarity. Therefore, new processes documented .

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: proportion incident light reflected surface water (\(R_l\)) calculated following Kirk et al. (2011): \[ \begin{align*}R_l = \frac{1}{2} *  \frac{sin^2(\theta_a - \theta_w)}{sin^2(\theta_a + \theta_w)} + \frac{1}{2} *\frac{tan^2(\theta_a - \theta_w)}{tan^2(\theta_a + \theta_w)}\\\end{align*} \] \(\theta_a\) solar zenith angle \(\theta_w\) angle refraction within water. \(\theta_w\) calculated following Snell’s law: \[ \frac{sin \theta_a}{sin \theta_w}= \frac{n_w}{n_a} \] \(n_w\) refractive index water \(n_a\) refractive index water. Air water different refractive properties value 1.33 ratio refractive indices air water good approximation freshwater wavelengths light within photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) (Kirk et al, 2011): \[ \frac{n_w}{n_a} = 1.33 \]

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Within water column light attenuates nonlinearly depth can described using Beer-Lambert equation: \[ I_z = I_{sub}e^{-{K_d}*z} \] light given depth (\(I_z\)) can calculated using light just water surface (\(I_{sub}\)), depth (\(z\)), extinction coefficient (\(K_d\)).

title: 4. Using aqua_light
text: Kirk, J. T. O. (2011). Light photosynthesis aquatic ecosystems (3rd ed.). Cambridge, UK 455 New York: Cambridge University Press.

what: An R Package for Estimating Stream Lighting Conditions
title: An R Package for Estimating Stream Lighting Conditions
text: StreamLight package contains two primary functions predicting photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) rivers streams: stream_light function predicts PAR water surface function channel geometry, riparian vegetation canopy structure phenology, sun-earth geometry. aqua_light function predicts PAR benthic surface. aqua_light function builds stream_light function allowing dynamic changes water width depth, well accounting reflection water surface attenuation within water column function depth clarity. Usage: use StreamLight package research please cite package. see suggested citation package, run citation(“StreamLight”) R prompt. Additionally, please cite appropriate manuscript associated function used: Savoy, P., Bernhardt, E., Kirk, L., Cohen, M. J., & Heffernan, J. B. (2021). seasonally dynamic model light stream surface. Freshwater Science, 40(April), 000–000. https://doi.org/10.1086/714270 Savoy, P., & Harvey, J. W. (2021). Predicting light regime controls primary productivity across CONUS river networks. Geophysical Research Letters, 48, e2020GL092149. https://doi.org/10.1029/2020GL092149 Package support: Development StreamLight package supported National Science Foundation (NSF) Macrosystem Program Grant (#EF 1442439) USGS Water Availability Use Science Program.

title: An R Package for Estimating Stream Lighting Conditions
text: See full StreamLight documentation individual articles use package. documentation page contains number articles detail usage StreamLight StreamLightUtils packages. Throughout articles terms emphasized using key. Key packages functions function arguments “column names” Individual articles go depth following subjects. Outline Download process NLDAS incoming shortwave radiation Covers downloading processing National Land Data Assimilation System (NLDAS) total incoming shortwave radiation (W m-2) using StreamLightUtils package. Download process MODIS LAI Covers downloading processing MODIS leaf area index (LAI, m2 m-2) using StreamLightUtils package. Using stream_light Covers creation driver files, parameter file, running stream_light. Using aqua_light Covers creation driver files, parameter file, running aqua_light.

title: An R Package for Estimating Stream Lighting Conditions

code: #Install the devtools package if you do not already have it      install.packages("devtools")  #Use the devtools packge to install StreamLightUtils   devtools::install_github("psavoy/StreamLightUtils")   devtools::install_github("psavoy/StreamLight") library("StreamLightUtils") library("StreamLight")

what: Calculate light absorption in pure water — absorption
title: Calculate light absorption in pure water — absorption
text: Calculates light absorption function depth clear water, using mean absorption coefficient pure water derived Pope & Fry 1997 Absorption spectrum ~380–700 nm pure water. II. Integrating cavity measurements. Effectively, assumes attenuation solely function absorption pure water. words, irradiance attenuation coefficient (Kd) equal just absorption coefficient pure water.

title: Calculate light absorption in pure water — absorption
code: absorption(driver, absorb_coef = 0.1521645)

title: Calculate light absorption in pure water — absorption
text: driver site driver file absorb_coef Absorption coefficient clear water. Defaults value derived Pope & Fry (1997) explained (absorb_coef = 0.1521645).

title: Calculate light absorption in pure water — absorption
text: Returns dataframe predicted light benthic surface irradiance attenuation coefficient.

what: Model to predict photosynthetically active radiation at the benthic surface. — aqua_light
title: Model to predict photosynthetically active radiation at the benthic surface. — aqua_light
text: function builds stream_light function adding several key components. First, makes modifications allow better handling dynamic wetted widths. Secondly, moves beyond making predictions stream surface includes influence surface reflection attenuation function depth clarity predict PAR benthic surface. Note aqua_light still output estimates PAR stream surface denoted column "PAR_surface".

title: Model to predict photosynthetically active radiation at the benthic surface. — aqua_light
code: aqua_light(   driver_file,   Lat,   Lon,   channel_azimuth,   bankfull_width,   BH,   TH,   overhang,   overhang_height,   x_LAD )

title: Model to predict photosynthetically active radiation at the benthic surface. — aqua_light
text: driver_file model driver file Lat site Latitude Lon site Longitude channel_azimuth Channel azimuth bankfull_width Bankfull width BH Bank height TH Tree height overhang Effectively canopy radius overhang_height height maximum canopy overhang (think canopy radius) x_LAD Leaf angle distribution, default = 1 WL Water level

title: Model to predict photosynthetically active radiation at the benthic surface. — aqua_light
text: Returns time series predicted photosynthetically active radiation
